title: Queue and circular buffer

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Queue and circular buffer; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A queue is a first-in-first-out abstract data type with enqueue at the back and dequeue at the front.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A circular buffer advances head and tail modulo capacity without shifting every queued item. Your goal is to implement and test enqueue, dequeue and wraparound behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Linked lists; Insertion and deletion. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A queue is a first-in-first-out abstract data type with enqueue at the back and dequeue at the front. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Array queues need circular indexing or compaction, and empty/full representation must not be ambiguous. The draft therefore rejects: An array queue must shift every remaining element after each dequeue.

includes: Specify FIFO behavior and implement a bounded or dynamically backed queue without shifting every item.
Implement and test enqueue, dequeue and wraparound behavior.

In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
excludes: Array queues need circular indexing or compaction, and empty/full representation must not be ambiguous.

outcomes: Implement and test enqueue, dequeue and wraparound behavior.

examples.title: Queue and circular buffer: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: A circular buffer advances head and tail modulo capacity without shifting every queued item.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A circular buffer advances head and tail modulo capacity without shifting every queued item.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use circular indexing to support constant-time enqueue and dequeue without repeated shifting.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Use circular indexing to support constant-time enqueue and dequeue without repeated shifting.

examples.title: Queue and circular buffer: implementation transfer

walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Array queues need circular indexing or compaction, and empty/full representation must not be ambiguous.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.
